narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, LSAT score of 36.50, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 2, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 51.6% chance you'll fail the bar—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment comparing your profile to others who've taken the exam. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass since it's very close to the typical student's score of 36.70. However, your Year 3 decile ranking of 2 was the next biggest factor pushing toward a failure prediction, as it's well below the average of 5.53 and signals weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2 reinforced this concern, ranking below the typical 5.71 and highlighting early academic challenges compared to most applicants. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, lower than the average 3.21, played a supporting role in this direction, though not as dominantly as your deciles. Full-time status aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%), so it didn't sway things much either way. Overall, while your LSAT offered some optimism, the model weighed your consistently low decile rankings as the key risks, setting you apart from typical students who rank higher and pass more reliably. This isn't set in stone—many in similar situations, including from Hispanic backgrounds, have succeeded by addressing weak spots. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, seeking targeted academic support to improve future rankings, or exploring post-baccalaureate programs to strengthen your GPA. You could also retake undergrad courses or gain relevant experience to rebuild your profile for the next application cycle. Keep pushing; your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer from an upper-middle background positions you well to overcome this.
